title: The Image on the Coin
subtitle: The flatterers encircle Christ with the question of the tribute to Caesar

stillness: Be still, and let the noise of flattering tongues fall silent.
Nothing is hidden from God, who searches the loins and the heart.
Come quietly before the Wisdom that no snare can catch.
gospel: The Pharisees went and took counsel how to ensnare him in his speech.
They sent their disciples to him, with the Herodians.
"Master, we know that you are truthful,
and you teach the way of God in truth,
and you care for no man, for you regard not the person of men.
Tell us therefore: is it lawful to give tribute to Caesar, or not?"
But Jesus, knowing their wickedness, said: "Why do you tempt me, you hypocrites?
Show me the coin of the tribute."
And they offered him a denarius.
And he said: "Whose is this image and inscription?"
Then he said: "Render therefore to Caesar the things that are Caesar's,
and to God the things that are God's."
behold: A man is more easily caught in his words than in his deeds.
Behold the disciples of the Pharisees, sent with the Herodians,
sent as though strangers, that they might deceive him more easily.
They encircle him like bees, carrying honey in their mouth and a sting in their tail.
With flattery they praise in him a threefold truth:
the truth of his life, the truth of his teaching, the truth of his justice.
They call him Master, that by honoring him they might deceive him,
for they praise the very ones they long to destroy.
But the fount of Wisdom fled their guile.
He answers not their words to their face, smoothly, but their consciences, sharply.
The Pharisees flattered, that they might destroy;
Jesus wounded, that he might save.
More useful is God angry than man well-pleased.
consider: Among all the sins of priests, the greatest is this: they regard persons, and not causes.
They despise the just and the poor, and they promote and exalt the wicked and the rich.
There are two kinds of persecutors: those who revile, and those who flatter,
and the tongue of the flatterer persecutes more than the hand of the slayer.
In refusing their praises, Christ teaches that no one ought to wish to be praised.
Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's: the tribute and the coin.
But render only what does not harm devotion, for what is evil is the tax not of Caesar but of the devil.
As Caesar demands the stamp of his own image, so God demands the soul marked with the light of his countenance.
As the denarius is sealed with the image of the king, so man is stamped to the image and likeness of God,
If you would owe nothing to the earthly king, leave all that is yours, and follow Christ.
We all renounce the world in words, but we do not renounce it in our heart.
How heavy are the chains: to promise to God, and not to pay.
Render what you promised while you are in this body,
before the exactor comes and casts you into prison.
prayer: teach me to understand and to beware the cunning of seducers;
and to keep always the truth of life, of doctrine, and of justice.
Grant that I may be marked with your image, not the enemy's,
that by leaving behind the things of the world, and clinging to you alone,
I may render back to you the things I have received from your grace,
Grant me also to flee the carnal and worldly way of life, which is barren,
and to follow the spiritual and heavenly one;
that, made new by the glory of the resurrection,
I may deserve to enjoy immortality with the Angels of God in heaven,
and the everlasting vision of you. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Sicut Cæsar exigit impressionem suæ imaginis, sic et Deus animam lumine vultus sui insignitam.
latinJewel.english: As Caesar demands the stamp of his own image, so also God demands the soul marked with the light of his countenance.
